title: Romans 6
subtitle: Dead to Sin, Alive in Christ

heb.paragraph: Paul’s baptismal theology in vv. 3–4 is participatory, not merely symbolic. Baptism into Christ is baptism into his death: the believer is co-crucified (synestaurōthē, v. 6), co-buried (synetaphēmen, v. 4), and will be co-raised (symphýtoi, v. 5) with Christ. The syn- prefix (‘with’) appears repeatedly, emphasising union. Baptism does not accomplish salvation mechanically but signifies and seals the believer’s participatory union with Christ in his death and resurrection.

The ‘old self’ (palaios anthrōpos) in v. 6 is not a component of the believer’s current identity but the pre-conversion self dominated by sin. Paul says it ‘was crucified with’ Christ (aorist, completed action), not that it is being gradually subdued. The body of sin (sōma tēs hamartias) is rendered powerless (katargēthē), not annihilated: sin’s dominion is broken even though its presence continues.

panels.ctx: Romans 6:1–14 addresses the logical objection to 5:20–21: if grace increases where sin increases, should we ‘go on sinning so that grace may increase’? Paul’s mē genoito (‘by no means!’) introduces the concept of union with Christ in death and resurrection as the definitive answer. The argument is both indicative (what has happened: the old self died with Christ) and imperative (what must follow: count yourselves dead to sin, v. 11). Paul’s ethics flow from identity: because believers have died with Christ, they must live accordingly. This is not legalism but the logic of new creation.

cross.note: Paul’s personal testimony of co-crucifixion: ‘I have been crucified with Christ and I no longer live, but Christ lives in me.’
Buried with Christ in baptism, raised with him through faith in the working of God.
If anyone is in Christ, the new creation has come: the old has gone, the new is here.

notes.note: The antinomian objection is absurd: shall we continue in sin? By no means! The question reveals a fundamental misunderstanding of grace. Grace does not license sin; it liberates from sin.
Baptism into Christ’s death is a real spiritual event, not merely a ritual. The believer’s old life has been terminated and a new life begun. The burial imagery emphasises the finality of death.

The ‘old self’ was crucified so that ‘the body ruled by sin might be done away with.’ The purpose is freedom: we are no longer slaves to sin because the slave-master relationship has been severed by death.
The imperative ‘count yourselves dead to sin’ (logizesthe) uses the same accounting verb from chapter 4. Believers must reckon as true what is in fact true: their death to sin in Christ.

Sin shall not be your master, because you are not under the law but under grace. This is not antinomianism but the announcement that grace accomplishes what law could not: actual freedom from sin’s dominion.

Calvin argues that the person who truly understands grace can never wish to continue in sin. Grace transforms the will, so that the justified person desires holiness rather than licence. Antinomianism is a contradiction in terms.

The crucifixion of the old self is a definitive event, not an ongoing process. Calvin distinguishes between the decisive break with sin’s dominion (past) and the progressive mortification of remaining sin (present).

The phrase ‘baptised into Christ Jesus’ (eis Christon Iēsoun) denotes incorporation into Christ’s person and saving events. The preposition eis indicates movement into union, not merely reference to Christ.

The verb katargeō (‘rendered powerless’ / ‘done away with’) does not mean ‘destroyed’ but ‘made inoperative’ or ‘nullified.’ Sin’s power is broken, though its presence remains until the final resurrection.

Moo argues that the key theological contribution of this passage is the concept of transference: through union with Christ, believers have been transferred from the realm of Adam (sin, death, law) to the realm of Christ (righteousness, life, grace). The imperatives of vv. 11–14 call believers to live out the reality of this transfer.

Moo takes ‘not under law but under grace’ as a salvation-historical statement about the two eras, not merely as a description of individual experience. The Mosaic era has given way to the era of grace in Christ.

Schreiner stresses that Paul’s response to the antinomian objection is not ethical exhortation but theological reality: the question is absurd because the justified person has already died to sin. You cannot continue living in a realm you have exited through death.

Schreiner reads the imperatives as flowing from the indicatives: because you are dead to sin (fact), count yourselves dead to sin (response). The imperative does not create the reality but appropriates it.

heb.paragraph: Paul deploys the slave metaphor (doulos) to argue that human beings are always under a master: either sin or righteousness. There is no autonomous middle ground. The metaphor is drawn from the institution of slavery in the Roman world, which Paul acknowledges is an imperfect analogy (‘I am using an example from everyday life,’ v. 19). But the theological point is sharp: the ‘freedom’ of sin is actually bondage, and the ‘slavery’ of righteousness is actually freedom.

The contrast in v. 23 between opsōnia (‘wages’) and charisma (‘gift’) is one of the most memorable in the NT. Opsōnia is a military term for a soldier’s pay-ration: what sin earns is death, paid out as wages owed. Charisma is a grace-gift, freely given and unearned: what God gives is eternal life in Christ Jesus. Sin pays what it owes; God gives what he chooses.

panels.ctx: Romans 6:15–23 addresses a second form of the antinomian objection: if we are not under law, shall we sin? Paul’s answer uses the slave-master metaphor to show that freedom from law does not mean moral autonomy. The passage moves from analogy (vv. 15–19) to application (vv. 20–23), contrasting the wages of sin (death) with the gift of God (eternal life). The famous conclusion in v. 23 is one of the most quoted verses in the Bible and encapsulates the entire soteriological argument of Romans 1–6.

cross.note: Jesus teaches that everyone who sins is a slave to sin, but the Son sets free — the same slavery/ freedom paradigm Paul develops.
You were bought at a price — the redemption metaphor that complements the slavery imagery.
Christ has set us free for freedom; do not submit again to a yoke of slavery.

notes.note: The second mē genoito in this chapter. Paul will not allow grace to become an excuse for sin under any formulation.
The principle is universal: you are a slave to whatever you obey. Obedience reveals your master. There is no neutrality in the spiritual war.
Paul’s apology for the slavery metaphor (‘because of the weakness of your flesh’) is a rare moment of rhetorical self-awareness. The metaphor is imperfect but pedagogically necessary.
The most concise statement of the gospel in Scripture. Sin pays wages (death); God gives a gift (eternal life). The contrast is absolute: earned vs. given, death vs. life.
Calvin notes the paradox: being ‘set free from sin’ means becoming ‘slaves to righteousness.’ True freedom is not autonomy but joyful submission to the right master.

Calvin emphasises that death is not arbitrary punishment but the natural consequence of sin — its wages, its earned outcome. Eternal life, by contrast, is entirely unmerited and springs from pure generosity.
The phrase ‘weakness of your flesh’ (astheneian tēs sarkos) may refer to the limited capacity of the readers to grasp spiritual truth, requiring Paul to use an everyday analogy.
The preposition en in ‘in Christ Jesus our Lord’ indicates the sphere in which the gift is received. Eternal life is not an abstract benefit but is found only in union with Christ.

Moo argues that the slave metaphor complements the death/resurrection metaphor of vv. 1–14. Both make the same point: believers have been transferred from one dominion to another. The slave imagery adds the dimension of obedience: the Christian life is not passive but active service to a new master.
Moo calls this verse a ‘theological epigram’ that compresses the entire gospel into two clauses. The asymmetry is deliberate: sin’s outcome is wages (earned); God’s outcome is a gift (free).

Schreiner emphasises that the transfer of lordship is definitive and irreversible. Believers ‘have been set free from sin’ (aorist passive) and ‘have become slaves of righteousness.’ The slave-transfer is not a gradual process but a decisive event accomplished in Christ.

Schreiner reads this verse as the climactic summary of chs. 5–6. The wages/gift contrast demonstrates that death and life are not symmetrical: death is earned; life is given. This asymmetry is the heart of grace.

themes.note: Holiness peaks at 9: this chapter is fundamentally about sanctification — the ethical transformation that justification produces. Mercy at 7 reflects the grace language throughout.

rows.label: vv.1–14
rows.text: Dead to Sin: Baptised into Christ’s Death

rows.label: vv.15–23
rows.text: Slaves to Righteousness: Freedom Through Service

lit.note: Romans 6 answers the antinomian objection to justification by faith. Two metaphors — death/resurrection (vv. 1–14) and slavery (vv. 15–23) — demonstrate that grace produces holiness, not licence.

ppl.name: Paul
ppl.role: Apostle to the Gentiles; author of thirteen NT letters
ppl.text: Paul (Saul of Tarsus) is introduced as a persecutor at Stephen’s stoning (Acts 7:58) and is dramatically converted on the Damascus road (Acts 9). He dominates Acts 13–28 across three missionary journeys, planting churches from Cyprus to Greece to Rome. His final journey to Rome as a prisoner frames Acts’ theological climax: the gospel reaching the empire’s capital, "boldly and without hindrance" (Acts 28:31).

ppl.name: Christ

trans.title: Comparing translations in Dead to Sin, Alive in Christ

translations.text: By no means! We are those who have died to sin; how can we live in it any longer?
By no means! How can we who died to sin still live in it?
because anyone who has died has been set free from sin.
For one who has died has been set free from sin.

discourse.thesis: Those united with Christ in baptism have died to sin and are alive to God — sin must not reign in their mortal bodies.

nodes.text: Shall we go on sinning so that grace may increase? By no means! We who died to sin — how can we live in it any longer?

Don't you know that all of us baptized into Christ were baptized into his death? We were buried with him through baptism into death, so that just as Christ was raised, we too may walk in newness of life. If united with him in death, we will certainly be united in resurrection.
Our old self was crucified with him so that the body of sin might be done away with — we should no longer be slaves to sin. Anyone who has died has been set free from sin.

If we died with Christ, we believe we will also live with him. Christ, raised from the dead, cannot die again — death no longer has mastery over him. He died to sin once for all; the life he lives, he lives to God.

Count yourselves dead to sin but alive to God in Christ Jesus. Do not let sin reign in your mortal body. Do not offer your members as instruments of wickedness, but offer yourselves to God as those alive from the dead. Sin shall not be your master — you are not under law but under grace.

Shall we sin because we are not under law but under grace? By no means! You are slaves to the one you obey — either sin leading to death, or obedience leading to righteousness. Thanks be to God that though you used to be slaves to sin, you have become slaves to righteousness.

Once you offered your members as slaves to impurity and lawlessness; now offer them as slaves to righteousness leading to holiness. When slaves of sin, you were free from righteousness — what benefit did you reap? Shame and death. But now, freed from sin and enslaved to God, the benefit is holiness and eternal life. The wages of sin is death, but the gift of God is eternal life in Christ Jesus our Lord.

discourse.note: Romans 6 answers the moral objection to grace: 'shall we sin more?' The twice-repeated 'By no means!' (μὴ γένοιτο) at vv. 2 and 15 anchors Paul's refutation. Baptism symbolizes union with Christ's death and resurrection.
